title: Count number of divisors of N
description: Given N, count how many positive integers divide N

examples.explanation: N=6. Initialize count=0. Loop i=1: 6%1=0 → count=1. i=2: 6%2=0 → count=2. i=3: 6%3=0 → count=3. i=4: 6%4=2 ≠0 → no change. i=5: 6%5=1 ≠0 → no change. i=6: 6%6=0 → count=4. Return 4.

N=17. Initialize count=0. Loop i=1: 17%1=0 → count=1. i=2: 17%2=1 ≠0 → no change. i=3: 17%3=2 ≠0 → no change. i=4: 17%4=1 ≠0 → no change. i=5: 17%5=2 ≠0 → no change. i=6: 17%6=5 ≠0 → no change. i=7: 17%7=3 ≠0 → no change. i=8: 17%8=1 ≠0 → no change. i=9: 17%9=8 ≠0 → no change. i=10: 17%10=7 ≠0 → no change. i=11: 17%11=6 ≠0 → no change. i=12: 17%12=5 ≠0 → no change. i=13: 17%13=4 ≠0 → no change. i=14: 17%14=3 ≠0 → no change. i=15: 17%15=2 ≠0 → no change. i=16: 17%16=1 ≠0 → no change. i=17: 17%17=0 → count=2. Return 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(N)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all numbers from 1 to N and check if each divides N. This is the most straightforward method but inefficient for large N.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors of N Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting all positive divisors of N by checking every number from 1 to N.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to counting divisors is the most intuitive method for beginners. The intuition is simple: a divisor of N is any positive integer i where N divided by i leaves no remainder. Since we need to count ALL divisors, we must check EVERY possible candidate from 1 to N. This exhaustive checking guarantees we won't miss any divisors, making the algorithm correct by construction. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a counter variable to 0. 2. Loop through i from 1 to N (inclusive). 3. For each i, check if N % i == 0 (using modulo operator). 4. If true, increment the counter. 5. After the loop, return the counter. Time complexity is O(N) because we perform N iterations, each with constant-time operations (modulo check and increment). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables regardless of N. This connects directly to the problem statement—it's the complete solution. Common mistakes include: starting the loop from 0 (causes division by zero error), using i < N instead of i <= N (misses N itself as divisor), or forgetting that 1 and N are always divisors. Real code pattern: def count_divisors(N): count = 0; for i in range(1, N+1): if N % i == 0: count += 1; return count.

examples.trace: N=6. Initialize count=0. Loop i=1: 6%1=0 → count=1. i=2: 6%2=0 → count=2. i=3: 6%3=0 → count=3. i=4: 6%4=2 ≠0 → no change. i=5: 6%5=1 ≠0 → no change. i=6: 6%6=0 → count=4. Return 4.

N=17. Initialize count=0. Loop i=1: 17%1=0 → count=1. i=2: 17%2=1 ≠0 → no change. i=3: 17%3=2 ≠0 → no change. i=4: 17%4=1 ≠0 → no change. i=5: 17%5=2 ≠0 → no change. i=6: 17%6=5 ≠0 → no change. i=7: 17%7=3 ≠0 → no change. i=8: 17%8=1 ≠0 → no change. i=9: 17%9=8 ≠0 → no change. i=10: 17%10=7 ≠0 → no change. i=11: 17%11=6 ≠0 → no change. i=12: 17%12=5 ≠0 → no change. i=13: 17%13=4 ≠0 → no change. i=14: 17%14=3 ≠0 → no change. i=15: 17%15=2 ≠0 → no change. i=16: 17%16=1 ≠0 → no change. i=17: 17%17=0 → count=2. Return 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Divisibility for a Single Number
ladders.desc: Determine if a given number i divides N without remainder.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach—it's the operation repeated N times in the full solution. The intuition: division has two outcomes—either it's exact (no remainder) or not. In programming, we use the modulo operator (%) to check this: if N % i == 0, then i divides N perfectly, meaning i is a divisor. Why does this work? Mathematically, a divisor d of N satisfies N = d × k for some integer k. The modulo operation computes the remainder when N is divided by i; if the remainder is 0, then N is exactly divisible by i. Step-by-step: 1. Take inputs N and i. 2. Compute remainder = N % i. 3. If remainder == 0, return True (or increment a counter in the full problem). 4. Otherwise, return False. Time complexity is O(1)—it's a single arithmetic operation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the condition checked in each iteration of the loop. Common mistakes: confusing / (division) with % (modulo), or not handling i=0 (which would cause division by zero error; but in our problem, i starts from 1). Real code pattern: def is_divisor(N, i): return N % i == 0.

examples.trace: Compute 10 % 3 = 1. Since 1 ≠ 0, return False.
Compute 10 % 5 = 0. Since 0 == 0, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate Through All Possible Divisors
ladders.desc: Loop from 1 to N to check each candidate number.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force method is the iteration that examines every integer from 1 to N as a potential divisor. The intuition: since we don't know in advance which numbers divide N, we must test them all. This exhaustive search is guaranteed to find all divisors because the definition of a divisor doesn't exclude any number in that range. Why iterate from 1 to N? Because any divisor of N must be at most N (since if d > N, then d × k > N for k ≥ 1). The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Set up a loop with a variable i starting at 1. 2. Continue while i ≤ N. 3. In each iteration, i represents a candidate divisor. 4. After checking i, increment i by 1. Time complexity is O(N) for the loop itself, assuming constant work per iteration. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This connects to L1 by providing the sequence of i values to check for divisibility. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (using i < N misses N; using i <= N is correct), or using while loops incorrectly (e.g., not incrementing i). Real code pattern: for i in range(1, N+1): # check divisibility for i.

examples.trace: Loop i=1: process i=1. i=2: process i=2. i=3: process i=3. i=4: process i=4. After i=4, loop ends because next i would be 5 > 4.
Loop i=1: process i=1. After i=1, loop ends because next i would be 2 > 1.

testCases.expected: Sequence: (empty, since loop from 1 to 0 doesn't execute)

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Remainder
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when N is divided by i.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation is fundamental to checking divisibility. Intuition: when you divide N by i, you get a quotient and a remainder. The remainder is what's left over after dividing as much as possible. If the remainder is 0, then i divides N exactly. Why is this useful? It's a computational way to test divisibility without performing full division. Step-by-step: Given N and i (with i > 0), the modulo operation N % i returns the remainder r such that 0 ≤ r < i and N = i × q + r for some integer q. In programming, % is the operator. Time and space complexity are O(1) as it's a basic arithmetic operation. This connects to L2 because in each iteration of the loop, we compute N % i to check divisibility. Common mistakes: misunderstanding that % gives remainder, not quotient; or not ensuring i is positive (but in our context, i starts from 1). Real code pattern: remainder = N % i.

examples.trace: 10 divided by 3 gives quotient 3 and remainder 1 because 10 = 3*3 + 1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
10 divided by 5 gives quotient 2 and remainder 0 because 10 = 5*2 + 0. So 10 % 5 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Comparison
ladders.desc: Perform division on integers and compare values.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic arithmetic and comparison operations needed for the modulo check. Intuition: to check if i divides N, we need to divide N by i and see if the result is an integer with no remainder. In programming, we break this into: 1. Integer division (// in Python) to get the quotient, though not directly used here, it's related. 2. Modulo operation (%) as in L3. 3. Comparison (==) to check if remainder is 0. Why are these basic? They are built-in operations in all programming languages. Step-by-step for our context: compute N % i, then compare the result to 0 using ==. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as it's the application of modulo and comparison together. Common mistakes: using = (assignment) instead of == (comparison), or confusing integer division with float division. Real code pattern: if N % i == 0: # do something.

examples.trace: Compute 6 % 2 = 0. Compare 0 == 0 → True.
Compute 6 % 4 = 2. Compare 2 == 0 → False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Positive Integers and Division
ladders.desc: Learn what positive integers and division mean in mathematics.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the entire problem. Intuition: positive integers are whole numbers greater than 0 (1, 2, 3, ...). Division is splitting a number into equal parts. For integers a and b (with b ≠ 0), we say b divides a if there exists an integer k such that a = b × k. In simpler terms, b divides a if you can multiply b by some whole number to get a exactly, with nothing left over. Why is this important? It defines what a divisor is. Step-by-step: 1. Identify N as a positive integer. 2. For a candidate divisor i (also positive integer), check if N/i is an integer. 3. If yes, i is a divisor. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 by providing the mathematical basis for the operations. Common mistakes: confusing division with other operations, or not considering only positive integers. Real code pattern: This is pre-code, but in words: 'divisor means N divided by i has no remainder'.

examples.trace: 6 divided by 2 is 3, which is an integer. So 2 is a divisor of 6.
6 divided by 4 is 1.5, which is not an integer. So 4 is not a divisor of 6.

approaches.name: Square Root Optimization O(√N)
approaches.summary: Iterate only up to √N, counting divisors in pairs. For each i that divides N, count both i and N/i (if i ≠ N/i). This reduces the search space dramatically.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors Using Square Root Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all positive divisors of N using O(√N) iteration.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete solution to count all positive divisors of N using square root optimization. The intuition is that divisors come in pairs: if i divides N, then N/i is also a divisor. By iterating only up to √N, we can count both divisors in each pair simultaneously, dramatically reducing the search space from O(N) to O(√N). The algorithm works by initializing a count to 0, then iterating i from 1 to √N (inclusive). For each i, if N % i == 0, we increment count by 1 for i. Then we check if i != N/i (to avoid double-counting perfect squares), and if so, increment count by 1 for N/i. The time complexity is O(√N) since we iterate up to √N, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the iteration logic itself. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle perfect squares (where i = N/i), using floating-point sqrt which can cause precision issues (better to use integer sqrt or i*i <= N), and off-by-one errors in the loop boundary. The algorithm is efficient for N up to about 10^12 in typical competitive programming constraints.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For i from 1 to floor(√N):
   a. If N % i == 0:
      i. count += 1
      ii. If i != N/i: count += 1
3. Return count

Time: O(√N), Space: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def count_divisors(N):
    count = 0
    i = 1
    while i * i <= N:
        if N % i == 0:
            count += 1
            if i != N // i:
                count += 1
        i += 1
    return count

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterate and Check Divisor Pairs
ladders.desc: The core loop that iterates up to √N and counts divisor pairs.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine implements the essential iteration logic of the square root optimization approach. The intuition is that we only need to check numbers up to the square root of N because every divisor d greater than √N has a corresponding divisor N/d that is less than √N. This creates a pairing relationship that allows us to count both divisors when we find one. The algorithm maintains a count variable and loops through i from 1 to √N. For each i, if i divides N (N % i == 0), we count i as a divisor. Then, we check if the paired divisor (N/i) is different from i - if it is, we count it too. This handles the pairing without double-counting perfect squares where i = N/i. The time complexity is O(√N) for the loop, and space is O(1). This feeds directly into the full problem (L0) as the main computational component. Common mistakes include: using i <= sqrt(N) with floating-point sqrt (causing precision errors), forgetting the i != N/i check (leading to double-counting for perfect squares), and incorrect loop boundaries (should be i*i <= N for integer safety). The sub-routine is the engine that makes the optimization work.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize count = 0
2. For i = 1 to floor(√N):
   a. If N % i == 0:
      i. count += 1
      ii. If i != N/i: count += 1
3. Return count

Time: O(√N), Space: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
def count_divisor_pairs(N):
    count = 0
    i = 1
    while i * i <= N:
        if N % i == 0:
            count += 1
            if i != N // i:
                count += 1
        i += 1
    return count

examples.trace:
Initialize count=0, i=1. While i*i <= 25 (i <= 5):
- i=1: 1*1=1 <=25, 25%1=0 → count=1, 1≠25/1=25 → count=2
- i=2: 2*2=4 <=25, 25%2=1 ≠0 → no change
- i=3: 3*3=9 <=25, 25%3=1 ≠0 → no change
- i=4: 4*4=16 <=25, 25%4=1 ≠0 → no change
- i=5: 5*5=25 <=25, 25%5=0 → count=3, 5=25/5=5 → no extra increment
- i=6: 6*6=36 >25, loop ends. Return count=3.
Divisor pairs: (1,25), (5,5) - 5 counted only once

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Pairing Property
ladders.desc: Understanding that divisors come in pairs symmetric around √N.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the mathematical insight that makes square root optimization possible: the divisor pairing property. The intuition is that if d is a divisor of N, then N/d is also a divisor of N. This creates pairs (d, N/d). When d < √N, then N/d > √N, and vice versa. Therefore, by finding all divisors up to √N, we automatically get all divisors greater than √N through these pairs. This reduces the search space from checking all numbers 1 to N (O(N)) to checking only up to √N (O(√N)). The key realization is that for every divisor i found where i ≤ √N, we get another divisor N/i (which may be equal to i in the case of perfect squares). This property holds because division is the inverse operation: if N = i * k, then both i and k are divisors. The time to understand this property is O(1) conceptually, though applying it requires O(√N) computation. This feeds into the key sub-routine (L1) as the theoretical foundation. Common mistakes include: not recognizing that the property breaks down if N is negative (but we're counting positive divisors), misunderstanding the boundary case when i = √N exactly (perfect squares), and not seeing why we only need to check up to √N. This insight is what transforms a naive O(N) approach into an efficient O(√N) solution.

Step-by-step understanding:
1. For any divisor d of N, there exists k such that N = d * k
2. Then k = N/d is also a divisor
3. If d < √N, then k = N/d > √N (since d*k = N and d < √N implies k > √N)
4. If d > √N, then k < √N
5. Therefore, all divisor pairs have one element ≤ √N and one ≥ √N
6. By finding all divisors ≤ √N, we get all divisors through pairing

Time: O(1) for the insight, Space: O(1)

Real code pattern: This is a conceptual understanding, not code, but it informs the loop: for i from 1 to √N, when i divides N, count both i and N/i.

examples.trace:
√18 ≈ 4.24, so check divisors up to 4:
- d=1: 1 divides 18, pair is (1,18) → 1≤√18, 18≥√18
- d=2: 2 divides 18, pair is (2,9) → 2≤√18, 9≥√18
- d=3: 3 divides 18, pair is (3,6) → 3≤√18, 6≥√18
- d=4: 4 does not divide 18
All divisors found: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Notice each pair has one element ≤√18 and one ≥√18.

√16 = 4 exactly, so check divisors up to 4:
- d=1: 1 divides 16, pair is (1,16) → 1≤4, 16≥4
- d=2: 2 divides 16, pair is (2,8) → 2≤4, 8≥4
- d=3: 3 does not divide 16
- d=4: 4 divides 16, pair is (4,4) → 4=4 (special case: perfect square)
All divisors: 1,2,4,8,16. The pair (4,4) shows the boundary case where both are equal to √N.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking Divisibility and Integer Square Root
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: testing if a divides b, and finding floor(√N).
ladders.explanation:
This level combines two essential building blocks needed for the square root optimization: checking divisibility and computing the integer square root. The intuition for divisibility is straightforward: a divides b if b % a == 0, meaning the remainder is zero when b is divided by a. This is the core operation we'll perform repeatedly in the loop. For the square root, we need the floor of √N (the largest integer i such that i*i ≤ N) to determine our loop boundary. Using floating-point sqrt() can cause precision issues with large integers, so it's safer to use integer arithmetic with i*i <= N as the loop condition. The algorithm for divisibility is a simple modulo check. For the square root boundary, we can either compute floor(sqrt(N)) using math libraries (with caution for precision) or use the i*i <= N condition directly in the loop. The time complexity for divisibility check is O(1), and for finding √N iteratively it would be O(√N), but we typically build it into the main loop. Space is O(1). These feed into the core logic (L2) as the operational components. Common mistakes include: using floating-point sqrt without proper rounding/flooring, integer overflow when computing i*i for large N (use 64-bit integers), and misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (but N is positive here). These are the basic tools that make the optimization practical.

Step-by-step for divisibility:
1. Given integers a and b (a > 0)
2. If b % a == 0, then a divides b
3. Otherwise, a does not divide b

Step-by-step for integer √N boundary:
Option A (pre-compute): sqrt_val = floor(sqrt(N)), loop i=1 to sqrt_val
Option B (inline): loop while i*i <= N

Time: O(1) per divisibility check, O(√N) if computing √N separately
Space: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
# Divisibility check
if N % i == 0:
    # i divides N

# Square root boundary (inline method, preferred)
while i * i <= N:
    # loop body
    i += 1

examples.trace: Compute b % a = 15 % 3 = 0. Since remainder is 0, 3 divides 15. Verification: 15 / 3 = 5 exactly.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental arithmetic: modulo operator (%) and integer comparisons.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the most basic operations required: the modulo operator and integer comparisons. The modulo operator (%) gives the remainder when one integer is divided by another. For positive integers a and b, a % b is the remainder of a divided by b, with 0 ≤ remainder < b. It's crucial for checking divisibility: if a % b == 0, then b divides a evenly. Integer comparisons (==, !=, <, >, ≤, ≥) are used to control loop boundaries and check conditions. The intuition is simple: these are elementary arithmetic operations that computers perform efficiently. For modulo, it's important to understand that it's not division but remainder calculation. For comparisons, they evaluate to true or false based on the numeric values. The time complexity for both operations is O(1) (constant time), and space is O(1). These feed into the building blocks (L3) as the atomic operations. Common mistakes include: confusing modulo with division, misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (but we're working with positive N), and off-by-one errors in comparisons (e.g., using < instead of ≤). These operations are so fundamental that they're built into all programming languages, but precise understanding is key for algorithm implementation.

Step-by-step for modulo:
1. Given integers a and b (b > 0)
2. Compute quotient q = floor(a / b)
3. Compute remainder r = a - (b * q)
4. a % b = r

Step-by-step for comparison:
1. Evaluate two integers
2. Apply comparison operator
3. Result is boolean (true/false)

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

Real code pattern (Python-style):
remainder = a % b  # e.g., 10 % 3 = 1
is_equal = (a == b)  # e.g., 5 == 5 → True
is_less = (a < b)    # e.g., 3 < 7 → True

examples.trace: 17 divided by 5: quotient = floor(17/5) = 3, remainder = 17 - (5*3) = 17 - 15 = 2. So 17 % 5 = 2. Verification: 17 = 5*3 + 2.

8 ≤ 8: 8 is less than or equal to 8 → True (since equal)
8 < 8: 8 is less than 8 → False (since not strictly less)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Positive Integers and Division
ladders.desc: Understanding positive integers and what it means for one number to divide another.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level introduces the basic concepts: positive integers and division. Positive integers are whole numbers greater than zero: 1, 2, 3, ... They are the building blocks of this problem since we're counting positive divisors. Division is the operation of splitting a number into equal parts. When we say 'a divides b' (written a | b), it means there exists an integer k such that b = a * k, with no remainder. For example, 3 divides 12 because 12 = 3 * 4. The intuition is that division checks if one number is a factor of another. This is the most basic prerequisite for the divisor counting problem. The concept doesn't involve an algorithm with time/space complexity, but understanding it is essential. This feeds into basic operations (L4) as the semantic foundation. Common mistakes include: confusing division with integer division (which truncates), not recognizing that 1 divides every integer, and forgetting that every integer divides itself. For beginners, it's important to practice with concrete examples to internalize these definitions. These concepts are typically taught in elementary arithmetic but are crucial for algorithmic thinking.

Step-by-step for 'a divides b':
1. Check if a and b are positive integers
2. Try to divide b by a
3. If the result is an integer (no fractional part), then a divides b
4. Equivalently, if b % a == 0

Examples:
- 2 divides 8 because 8/2 = 4 (integer)
- 3 does not divide 10 because 10/3 ≈ 3.33 (not integer)

Real code pattern: This is conceptual, but in code we'd express it as b % a == 0.

examples.trace: Check: 20 ÷ 4 = 5. Since 5 is an integer (no remainder), 4 divides 20. We can also verify: 20 = 4 * 5 exactly.
Check: 30 ÷ 7 ≈ 4.2857... This is not an integer (it has a fractional part). Therefore, 7 does not divide 30. Verification: 7 * 4 = 28 (remainder 2), 7 * 5 = 35 (too big).

approaches.name: Prime Factorization O(√N)
approaches.summary: Factorize N into prime factors, then use the divisor count formula: if N = p₁^a₁ * p₂^a₂ * ..., the number of divisors is (a₁+1)*(a₂+1)*.... This is efficient and mathematically elegant.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Divisors via Prime Factorization
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to count all positive divisors of N using prime factorization and divisor formula.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: every integer N > 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime powers. The key insight is that divisors correspond to combinations of these prime powers. If N = p₁^a₁ * p₂^a₂ * ... * pₖ^aₖ, then any divisor d can be written as p₁^b₁ * p₂^b₂ * ... * pₖ^bₖ where 0 ≤ bᵢ ≤ aᵢ. The number of choices for each exponent bᵢ is (aᵢ + 1), so total divisors = ∏(aᵢ + 1). The algorithm first factorizes N by iterating i from 2 to √N. For each i dividing N, count its exponent a by repeatedly dividing N by i while N % i == 0. After the loop, if N > 1, it's a prime factor with exponent 1. Then compute product of (exponent + 1). Time complexity is O(√N) because we iterate up to √N. Space is O(1) using only a few variables. This connects to L1 by using the prime factorization subroutine and applying the divisor formula. Common mistakes include: not handling N=1 separately (has 1 divisor), forgetting to check if remaining N > 1 after loop (last prime factor), integer overflow with large N, and inefficiently checking all numbers instead of only up to √N. Real code pattern: def count_divisors(N): if N==1: return 1; result=1; i=2; while i*i <= N: if N%i==0: count=0; while N%i==0: N//=i; count+=1; result*=(count+1); i+=1; if N>1: result*=2; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=12, result=1. Step 2: i=2, 2*2=4 ≤ 12, N%2=0 → factor found. Step 3: Count exponent of 2: while 12%2=0: count=1, N=6; 6%2=0: count=2, N=3; 3%2≠0 → stop. result*=(2+1)=3. Step 4: i=3, 3*3=9 ≤ 3? No, loop ends. Step 5: N=3>1 → last prime factor with exponent 1, result*=2 → 6. Step 6: Return 6.

Step 1: N=100, result=1. Step 2: i=2, 4 ≤ 100, N%2=0 → factor. Step 3: Count exponent: 100%2=0: count=1, N=50; 50%2=0: count=2, N=25; 25%2≠0 → stop. result*=(2+1)=3. Step 4: i=3, 9 ≤ 25, N%3=25%3=1≠0 → skip. Step 5: i=4, 16 ≤ 25, N%4=25%4=1≠0 → skip. Step 6: i=5, 25 ≤ 25, N%5=0 → factor. Step 7: Count exponent: 25%5=0: count=1, N=5; 5%5=0: count=2, N=1; stop. result*=(2+1)=9. Step 8: i=6, 36 ≤ 1? No, loop ends. Step 9: N=1 not >1 → done. Step 10: Return 9.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization with Exponents
ladders.desc: Factorize N into prime factors and record their exponents.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine decomposes N into its prime factors and counts how many times each prime divides N. The intuition is based on trial division: any composite number N has a prime factor ≤ √N. We iterate i from 2 to √N. For each i, if i divides N, we repeatedly divide N by i while counting the exponent. After exhausting all i, if N > 1, it's the last prime factor with exponent 1. This works because dividing N by i removes that factor, ensuring we only check prime factors (if i is composite, its prime factors would have been removed earlier). Time complexity is O(√N) in worst case (when N is prime). Space is O(k) to store factors and exponents, but we can process on-the-fly for divisor count. This feeds into L0 by providing the exponents needed for the divisor formula. Common mistakes: not updating N during division (leading to infinite loops), checking i beyond √N unnecessarily, and mishandling N=1. Real code pattern: def prime_factors(N): factors={}; i=2; while i*i <= N: if N%i==0: count=0; while N%i==0: N//=i; count+=1; factors[i]=count; i+=1; if N>1: factors[N]=1; return factors.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=18, i=2, 4 ≤ 18. Step 2: 18%2=0 → factor 2. Step 3: Count exponent: while 18%2=0: count=1, N=9; 9%2≠0 → stop. Record 2^1. Step 4: i=3, 9 ≤ 9. Step 5: 9%3=0 → factor 3. Step 6: Count exponent: while 9%3=0: count=1, N=3; 3%3=0: count=2, N=1; stop. Record 3^2. Step 7: i=4, 16 ≤ 1? No, loop ends. Step 8: N=1 not >1 → done. Step 9: Return {2:1, 3:2}.

Step 1: N=49, i=2, 4 ≤ 49, 49%2=1≠0 → skip. Step 2: i=3, 9 ≤ 49, 49%3=1≠0 → skip. Step 3: i=4, 16 ≤ 49, 49%4=1≠0 → skip. Step 4: i=5, 25 ≤ 49, 49%5=4≠0 → skip. Step 5: i=6, 36 ≤ 49, 49%6=1≠0 → skip. Step 6: i=7, 49 ≤ 49, 49%7=0 → factor 7. Step 7: Count exponent: while 49%7=0: count=1, N=7; 7%7=0: count=2, N=1; stop. Record 7^2. Step 8: i=8, 64 ≤ 1? No, loop ends. Step 9: N=1 not >1 → done. Step 10: Return {7:2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Count Formula from Prime Exponents
ladders.desc: Given prime exponents, compute total divisors using product of (exponent+1).
ladders.explanation: This is the mathematical heart of the approach. If N = p₁^a₁ * p₂^a₂ * ... * pₖ^aₖ, then the number of positive divisors is (a₁+1)*(a₂+1)*...*(aₖ+1). The intuition: each divisor can be formed by choosing an exponent for each prime factor from 0 to aᵢ. For prime pᵢ, there are (aᵢ+1) choices (0,1,...,aᵢ). Since choices are independent across primes, multiply them. This formula is efficient because it avoids enumerating divisors. The algorithm takes a dictionary of prime→exponent pairs and multiplies (exp+1). Time O(k) where k is number of distinct primes, space O(1). This connects to L1 by using the exponents from factorization. Common mistakes: forgetting to add 1 to exponent, multiplying exponents instead of (exp+1), and not handling empty input (N=1 gives product over empty set = 1). Real code pattern: def divisor_count_from_exponents(prime_exponents): result=1; for exp in prime_exponents.values(): result*=(exp+1); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Exponents: 2→2, 3→1. Step 2: Compute (2+1)=3 for prime 2. Step 3: Compute (1+1)=2 for prime 3. Step 4: Multiply: 3*2=6. Step 5: Return 6.
Step 1: Exponents: 5→3. Step 2: Compute (3+1)=4. Step 3: Multiply (only one factor): 4. Step 4: Return 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trial Division to Find a Prime Factor
ladders.desc: Find the smallest prime factor of N by checking divisibility from 2 upward.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept finds one prime factor of N using trial division. The intuition: if N is composite, it has a prime factor ≤ √N. We check integers i starting from 2. If i divides N, return i as a factor. If no factor found by √N, then N is prime. This works because any factor of a composite number is either prime or has a smaller prime factor. By checking in increasing order, we find the smallest prime factor. Time O(√N) in worst case. Space O(1). This feeds into L1 by being the core step in factorization loop. Common mistakes: checking beyond √N (unnecessary), not handling N=1 (no factor), and inefficiently checking even numbers after 2 (can optimize but not required). Real code pattern: def smallest_prime_factor(N): if N==1: return None; i=2; while i*i <= N: if N%i==0: return i; i+=1; return N.

examples.trace: Step 1: N=15, i=2, 4 ≤ 15, 15%2=1≠0 → skip. Step 2: i=3, 9 ≤ 15, 15%3=0 → factor found. Step 3: Return 3.

Step 1: N=29, i=2, 4 ≤ 29, 29%2=1≠0 → skip. Step 2: i=3, 9 ≤ 29, 29%3=2≠0 → skip. Step 3: i=4, 16 ≤ 29, 29%4=1≠0 → skip. Step 4: i=5, 25 ≤ 29, 29%5=4≠0 → skip. Step 5: i=6, 36 ≤ 29? No, loop ends. Step 6: No factor found, so N is prime. Return 29.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Divisibility and Reduce N
ladders.desc: Given N and a divisor d, check if d divides N and update N by dividing.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation tests if d divides N (i.e., N % d == 0) and if so, performs integer division N //= d. The intuition: divisibility check is core to factorization. The modulo operator % gives remainder; if 0, d is a factor. Dividing N by d removes that factor, reducing the problem size. This is repeated in factorization to count exponents. Time O(1) per operation. Space O(1). This feeds into L3 as the inner step in trial division. Common mistakes: using floating-point division instead of integer, not updating N correctly, and infinite loops if condition not checked properly. Real code pattern: def check_and_reduce(N, d): if N % d == 0: count=0; while N % d == 0: N //= d; count+=1; return (True, N, count); else: return (False, N, 0).

examples.trace: Step 1: N=12, d=2. Step 2: Check 12%2=0 → divisible. Step 3: Initialize count=0. Step 4: While 12%2=0: N=12//2=6, count=1. Step 5: While 6%2=0: N=6//2=3, count=2. Step 6: 3%2=1≠0 → stop. Step 7: Return (True, N=3, count=2).
Step 1: N=7, d=3. Step 2: Check 7%3=1≠0 → not divisible. Step 3: Return (False, N=7, count=0).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers, composite numbers, and basic divisibility rules.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. A composite number has more than two divisors. Divisibility: a divides b (a | b) if b % a == 0. Key intuition: every integer >1 is either prime or can be broken into prime factors (fundamental theorem of arithmetic). This foundation is essential because our approach relies on prime factorization. The algorithm involves checking divisibility by small numbers to find primes. Time/space not applicable here as conceptual. This feeds into L4 by providing the context for divisibility checks. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime (it's not), confusing factors with multiples, and not understanding modulo operation. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<=1: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: 5 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1 and 5 only. Step 3: No other numbers between 2 and √5≈2.2 divide 5. Step 4: Thus, 5 is prime.
Step 1: 6 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1,2,3,6. Step 3: Has divisors other than 1 and itself. Step 4: Thus, 6 is composite.
